topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the locker room was a physical weight pressing down on Alex. He stared at the polished floor, the reflection of his own face fractured in the surface. He had poured every ounce of his energy, every grueling early morning practice, into this moment, into the belief that he belonged. Now, the official announcement echoed in the cavernous space, a sound that seemed to drain the color from the fluorescent lights. When his name wasn't called, the air felt thin, as if he couldn't draw a full breath. He slowly pulled his jersey off, the fabric feeling suddenly coarse and alien against his skin. His hands trembled as he folded it neatly, a small, tight knot forming in his stomach. "Right," he managed, the word coming out as a dry rasp. He turned and walked toward the exit, each step feeling deliberate, heavy with unspoken disappointment.

[story 2]

"No. That can't be right." Maya whispered, her voice tight with disbelief as she reread the email for the tenth time. The words confirmed the crushing reality: she wasn't making the squad. A strange, hot flush crept up her neck, spreading across her cheeks until she felt feverish. She gripped the edge of the worn wooden bench, knuckles whitening beneath the pressure. Every glance from her teammates seemed sharp, assessing, as if she were suddenly transparent and flawed. She could feel the heat radiating off her skin, a stark contrast to the icy dread pooling in her chest. "Fine," she said, the sound surprisingly steady, though the tremor in her voice betrayed the turmoil churning inside. She pushed herself up, the movement stiff, and walked toward the door, needing only the anonymity of the hallway.

[story 3]

The applause for the chosen group sounded distant, muffled, as if coming from another planet. Liam stood near the edge of the field, watching the procession move toward the entrance. His chest felt constricted, a vise tightening around his ribs. He watched the familiar faces, the camaraderie he’d worked so hard to earn, moving past him toward the center of attention. A dull ache settled deep in his shoulders, a heavy, leaden sensation that made lifting his arms feel like an impossible feat. When the final group entered, the sound seemed to amplify, highlighting the gulf that had just opened between him and them. He didn't move to join the periphery; instead, he turned his back on the spectacle, focusing intently on the scuff marks in the dirt, trying to anchor himself to something solid while the world spun just a little too fast.
